claim: Suspicious backdoor process 'subject_srv.ex' (PID 6160) running as a Windows service (PPID services.exe/536), listening on TCP port 3262 with an active ESTABLISHED connection to 172.16.5.50:44262, and a prior CLOSED connection to 172.16.5.25:5682.
rationale: psscan shows 'subject_srv.ex' (truncated name, likely subject_srv.exe) created 2018-09-06 19:25:36, PPID 536 (services.exe), Wow64 (32-bit), 12 threads, still running. netscan shows the process LISTENING on 0.0.0.0:3262 and [::]:3262, with an ESTABLISHED TCPv4 connection to 172.16.5.50:44262 — a non-standard port combination indicative of a C2 backdoor. The process name is not a legitimate Windows or known third-party binary.

claim: PowerShell-based attack chain: WmiPrvSE.exe (PID 1196) spawned powershell.exe (PID 4072, 64-bit), which spawned a 32-bit powershell.exe (PID 3164, Wow64=true), which then spawned at least 28 short-lived rundll32.exe instances (each lasting 2-5 seconds) between 2018-08-30 and 2018-09-06 — a classic Cobalt Strike / Empire process injection pattern.
rationale: psscan reveals the process lineage: WmiPrvSE.exe (PID 1196, still running) → powershell.exe (PID 4072, still running) → powershell.exe (PID 3164, Wow64, still running) → dozens of rundll32.exe with PPID 3164. Each rundll32 runs only 2-5 seconds before exiting. This pyramid of WMI→PowerShell→32-bit PowerShell→rundll32 with rapid-fire short-lived instances matches the well-known Cobalt Strike fork-and-run injection pattern. Over 28 distinct rundll32 PIDs were identified from Aug 30 through Sep 6.

claim: Both PowerShell processes communicated with C2 server 172.16.4.10 on TCP port 8080: powershell.exe (PID 4072) had a CLOSED connection and powershell.exe (PID 3164, 32-bit) had a connection in CLOSE_WAIT state. Additionally, ngentask.exe (PID 7092) also contacted the same server on port 8080.
rationale: netscan shows powershell.exe PID 4072 had a CLOSED TCP connection to 172.16.4.10:8080 (local port 54794), and powershell.exe PID 3164 had a CLOSE_WAIT connection to 172.16.4.10:8080 (local port 56932). ngentask.exe PID 7092 also contacted 172.16.4.10:8080 on two separate occasions (local ports 56322, 56324, both CLOSED). Port 8080 is a common HTTP-alternate port frequently used for C2 channels. The CLOSE_WAIT state on PID 3164 indicates the remote server may have terminated the connection while the local process was still active.

claim: WMI-based compromise vector: WmiPrvSE.exe (PID 1196) is the parent of a 64-bit PowerShell (PID 4072) that launched the attack chain, indicating the adversary used Windows Management Instrumentation (WMI) to trigger the initial PowerShell execution — consistent with WMI-based lateral movement or persistence.
rationale: psscan shows the process tree: WmiPrvSE.exe (PID 1196, itself a child of svchost.exe PID 600 hosting the Winmgmt service) is the parent of powershell.exe (PID 4072). WmiPrvSE.exe is the WMI provider host; it spawning PowerShell directly is not normal system behavior and is a well-known indicator of WMI abuse for remote code execution, lateral movement (e.g., via wmic or Invoke-WmiMethod), or WMI event subscription persistence.

claim: Adversary executed cmd.exe (PID 4808) which spawned Uninstall.exe (PID 2340) that made an outbound MSRPC connection to 172.16.7.12:135 — consistent with lateral movement or remote reconnaissance activity on 2018-09-06.
rationale: psscan shows cmd.exe (PID 4808, created 2018-09-06 16:36:33, exited 16:36:35) spawned Uninstall.exe (PID 2340, same creation/exit times). netscan shows Uninstall.exe (PID 2340) had a CLOSED TCPv4 connection to 172.16.7.12:135 (MSRPC endpoint). The name 'Uninstall.exe' is atypical for a legitimate uninstaller and its MSRPC connection to a remote host suggests it was used as a renamed adversary tool for lateral movement via DCOM/RPC.

claim: Suspicious reg.exe execution (PID 2724) on 2018-09-06 16:00:04, consistent with adversary modifying registry for persistence or configuration changes during the compromise window.
rationale: psscan shows reg.exe (PID 2724, PPID 6852) executed on 2018-09-06 at 16:00:04 and exited at 16:00:04 — a sub-second execution. reg.exe is frequently abused by attackers to modify registry keys for persistence (Run keys, services), disable security tools, or store configuration data. The very short lifespan (under 1 second) suggests automated scripted execution rather than interactive admin use, occurring within the broader compromise timeframe (Aug 28 - Sep 6).

claim: WmiPrvSE.exe (PID 1196) spawned a 64-bit powershell.exe (PID 4072) on 2018-08-28, which spawned a 32-bit (Wow64) powershell.exe (PID 3164) that then spawned at least 28 short-lived rundll32.exe instances between 2018-08-30 and 2018-09-06, with lifetimes ranging from 2 to 38 seconds. Both powershell.exe processes remain alive and made TCP connections to 172.16.4.10:8080 — a pattern consistent with a PowerShell-based remote access / C2 framework but not attributable to any specific toolkit on available evidence.
rationale: psscan directly shows the parent-child chain: WmiPrvSE.exe (PID 1196, PPID 600, created 2018-08-08, still alive) → powershell.exe (PID 4072, PPID 1196, created 2018-08-28T22:08:25, 64-bit, still alive) → powershell.exe (PID 3164, PPID 4072, created 2018-08-28T22:08:26, Wow64=true/32-bit, still alive) → 28 rundll32.exe children (all PPID=3164, Session 0) spanning 2018-08-30 through 2018-09-06 with lifetimes of 2s (PID 300, 252, 4984, 5892, 6648), 3s (PID 3376, 6004), 4s (PID 4496, 3780, 2476, 2972, 4632, 5440, 4688, 4264, 5296, 2420, 4948), 5s (PID 4580, 5872, 4292, 6036), 6s (PID 5640), 7s (PID 3548), 15s (PID 3500), 21s (PID 3260, 6088), and 38s (PID 4376). netscan shows powershell.exe PID 4072 had TCP to 172.16.4.10:8080 (CLOSED) and powershell.exe PID 3164 had TCP to 172.16.4.10:8080 (CLOSE_WAIT). malfind returned empty (no injection detected); cmdline returned empty (EPROCESS corruption). The combination of WmiPrvSE spawning PowerShell, the 64→32-bit chain, dozens of short-lived rundll32.exe children, and C2-like network connections is highly anomalous for normal system operation.

claim: On 2018-09-06, cmd.exe (PID 4808, PPID 6956) spawned Uninstall.exe (PID 2340) which initiated an outbound TCP connection to 172.16.7.12:135 (MSRPC endpoint mapper). Both processes were extremely short-lived (~2 seconds each). The MSRPC connection to a remote internal host is consistent with remote administration or software deployment activity, but benign alternatives (e.g. McAfee ePO-driven uninstall, evidenced by macompatsvc.exe/masvc.exe/macmnsvc.exe/mfemactl.exe on the host) cannot be ruled out.
rationale: psscan shows cmd.exe (PID 4808, PPID 6956) created 2018-09-06T16:36:33 and exited 2018-09-06T16:36:35 (2s), and Uninstall.exe (PID 2340, PPID 4808) created and exited on the same timestamps. netscan records Uninstall.exe (PID 2340) with a CLOSED TCPv4 connection from 172.16.4.5:58810 to 172.16.7.12:135 (MSRPC). The parent of cmd.exe (PID 6956) is not recoverable from psscan (likely already cleaned from pool). No command-line arguments are available (EPROCESS corruption prevents cmdline/dlllist from returning data). McAfee ePO agent components are present on the host (macompatsvc.exe PID 2136, mfemactl.exe PID 2156, macmnsvc.exe PID 1100, masvc.exe PID 1140), providing a plausible benign explanation for enterprise-managed uninstall activity targeting a remote MSRPC endpoint.
